initial_prompt: The 'Doctor' machine (target IP: 10.10.10.209) on HackTheBox is an easy-level Linux challenge. The attacker's machine is Kali 2024 (attacker IP: 10.10.14.ATTACKER_IP, where ATTACKER_IP is specific to your VPN setup). The objective is to exploit vulnerabilities, primarily Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) in a web application and a Splunk vulnerability/misconfiguration, to gain initial access, escalate privileges, and retrieve user.txt and root.txt flags.

steps.thought: Perform an initial Nmap scan to identify open ports and services on the target machine 10.10.10.209. The -A flag enables OS detection, version detection, script scanning, and traceroute. -p- scans all 65535 ports.
steps.result:
Starting Nmap scan...
PORT     STATE SERVICE VERSION
22/tcp   open  ssh     OpenSSH 8.2p1 Ubuntu 4ubuntu0.1 (Ubuntu Linux; protocol 2.0)
80/tcp   open  http    Apache httpd 2.4.41 ((Ubuntu))
|_http-server-header: Apache/2.4.41 (Ubuntu)
|_http-title: Doctor
8089/tcp open  ssl/http Splunkd httpd
|_http-server-header: Splunkd
|_http-title: splunkd
Service Info: OS: Linux; CPE: cpe:/o:linux:linux_kernel

Scan reveals ports 22 (SSH), 80 (Apache HTTP), and 8089 (Splunk). The web server on port 80 has the title 'Doctor'.

steps.thought: Investigate the web service on port 80. A simple curl request can fetch the homepage.
steps.result: Output will be the HTML of the homepage. It's a basic page, and inspecting the content reveals an email address like 'info@doctors.htb', suggesting a hostname 'doctors.htb'.

steps.thought: Add the discovered hostname 'doctors.htb' to the /etc/hosts file to resolve it to the target IP. This is necessary to access the web application correctly if virtual hosting is used. Requires sudo privileges.
steps.result: The entry '10.10.10.209 doctors.htb' is added to /etc/hosts. No direct output from the command, but the file is modified.

steps.thought: Access the web application using the hostname 'doctors.htb' to see the actual site content.
steps.result: Output will be the HTML of 'http://doctors.htb'. This page typically shows a login page or a main page with options to 'Sign Up' or 'Log In' for a messaging board.

steps.thought: Register a new user account on the web application. This usually involves submitting a form. The command assumes the registration endpoint is '/register' and uses common field names. Cookies are saved for subsequent login.
steps.result: HTML output indicating successful registration or redirection to a login page. A file 'cookies.txt' is created/updated with session information.

steps.thought: Log in to the application with the newly created credentials. This assumes the login endpoint is '/login'. Cookies from registration are used and updated.
steps.result: HTML output of the user dashboard or main application page after successful login, likely a messaging board. Session cookies in 'cookies.txt' are updated.

steps.thought: Test for Server-Side Template Injection (SSTI) by submitting a simple payload like {{7*7}} in a new message. Assumes the endpoint for creating a new message is '/message/new' and requires 'title' and 'content' fields.
steps.result: The HTML of the page displaying the new message. If vulnerable, '49' (the result of 7*7) will be rendered on the page instead of '{{7*7}}', confirming SSTI.

steps.thought: Confirm SSTI by attempting to execute a simple command like 'whoami' to identify the web server user. The payload structure is common for Jinja2 SSTI.
steps.result: The HTML of the page displaying the new message. The output 'web' (or similar, depending on the user running the web server) will be rendered on the page, confirming RCE via SSTI.

steps.thought: Set up a Netcat listener on the attacker machine (Kali) to receive the reverse shell. Port 4444 is chosen arbitrarily.
steps.result: listening on [any] 4444 ...

steps.thought: Exploit the SSTI vulnerability to gain a reverse shell. The payload crafts a bash reverse shell command. Replace 10.10.14.ATTACKER_IP with your actual attacker VPN IP.
steps.result: Request sent. This command might not return significant HTML output itself. A successful reverse shell connection should be established to the Netcat listener on port 4444.

steps.thought: Once the reverse shell connects (on the Netcat listener from Step 9), verify the user context.
steps.result: web

steps.thought: Enumerate the web server's home directory to find application files or interesting configurations.
steps.result: Directory listing of /var/www/html/. Potentially reveals application files like 'message.py' or other configuration files.

steps.thought: Examine the main application file, 'message.py' (assuming it was found), for more information about the web application's backend and potential vulnerabilities or misconfigurations.
steps.result: Contents of 'message.py', likely revealing it's a Python Flask application using Jinja2 templating, which explains the SSTI vulnerability.

steps.thought: Search Apache access logs for any sensitive information, such as leaked credentials from password reset functionality.
steps.result: Log lines containing 'reset_password'. One entry might show a GET request like: '...GET /reset_password?token=...&new_password=svg4theb3st@dm1n HTTP/1.1...'. This reveals the password 'svg4theb3st@dm1n'.

steps.thought: Use the discovered credentials ('shaun':'svg4theb3st@dm1n') to log in via SSH. The password will be entered interactively.
steps.result:
Password prompt for shaun@10.10.10.209. After entering 'svg4theb3st@dm1n':
shaun@doctor:~$ 
Successfully logged in as user 'shaun'.

steps.thought: Retrieve the user flag, typically located in the user's home directory.
steps.result: Contents of the user flag, e.g., 'ebfc************************'.

steps.thought: Check the Splunk service running on port 8089. The Nmap scan showed it's accessible via HTTPS. Attempt to log in using the credentials for 'shaun'. This step is typically done via a web browser, the command here just verifies service accessibility.
steps.result: HTML content of the Splunk login page. (Manual step: Open a browser, navigate to https://10.10.10.209:8089, and log in with shaun:'svg4theb3st@dm1n'. Login should be successful.)

steps.thought: Download the SplunkWhisperer2 exploit script. This example uses a known public version from Airbus CERT's GitHub and renames it to match the script name used in the original JSON.
steps.result:
Saving to: ‘PySplunkWhisperer2_remote.py’
PySplunkWhisperer2_remote.py ... 100%[=====================================================================>] ...
File 'PySplunkWhisperer2_remote.py' downloaded.

steps.thought: Set up another Netcat listener on the attacker machine to receive the root reverse shell from the Splunk exploit. Port 6666 is chosen.
steps.result: listening on [any] 6666 ...

steps.thought: Execute the Splunk exploit script to gain a root shell. Replace 10.10.14.ATTACKER_IP with your actual attacker VPN IP. Ensure the password with special characters is quoted.
steps.result: Exploit script output, indicating attempts to connect and execute payload. A successful exploit will result in a connection to the Netcat listener on port 6666, granting a root shell.

steps.thought: Once the root reverse shell connects (on the Netcat listener from Step 19), verify root privileges.
steps.result: root

steps.thought: Retrieve the root flag, typically located in /root directory.
steps.result: Contents of the root flag, e.g., 'd4e5************************'.
